What is the second step in the rating and ranking process as defined by the DEOH?
✓ B is correct. The three steps of rating and ranking are: (1) apply quality ranking factors, (2) administer assessment tool(s), (3) apply veterans' preference. Determining minimum qualifications is part of the earlier application review process (Unit 5), not the rating and ranking process. (DEOH Ch.5 B, p. 5-3)

4. A-C-E Quality Level Rating and Augmentation Points

▼

Three Quality Levels

LevelDefinitionTypical Points
AExceptional experience90 points
CGood experience80 points
EMinimally qualifying70 points

Augmentation Points & Tie-Breaking

When many applicants have tied scores, refine by assigning additional points based on job-related competencies/KSAs, quality ranking factors, or higher proficiency on selective factors (DEOH p. 5-7).

  • Levels C and E: Maximum 9 points (cannot raise to next level)
  • Level A: Maximum 10 points (no higher level to breach)

Under traditional rule of three, when only a few applicants are in the quality levels being considered, you may use a tie-breaking procedure based on job-related criteria for selection among applicants within a quality level (DEOH p. 5-7). Under category rating, see Appendix M (Random Referral) for tie-breaking guidance.

⚠ Exam Trap: Veterans' preference points are the one exception that may raise a score above a quality level ceiling. Augmentation points from competency proficiency may not.
📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.5 Section B, pp. 5-7 to 5-8. The A-C-E (Quality Level Rating) method assigns quality levels: A (highest), C (mid-range), E (minimally acceptable) and converts to numerical scores. Augmentation points (additional points for unmeasured job-related competencies) are on p. 5-8. For random referral (tie-breaking), see Appendix M. The transmutation tables (converting raw scores to ratings between 70 and 100) are in Appendix I.
✦ Knowledge Check
A Level C applicant (80 points) receives 10 augmentation points for exceptional proficiency, totaling 90. Permissible?
✓ A is correct. "For quality levels 'C' and 'E,' the maximum number of points a rating could be augmented is 9 points." 80+10=90 would breach Level A. Only veterans' preference points may cross level boundaries. (DEOH Ch.5 B, p. 5-8)

5. Category Rating — Authority, Defining Categories & Requirements

▼

Legal Authority

Chief Human Capital Officers Act of 2002 (Title XIII, Homeland Security Act), codified at 5 U.S.C. § 3319 (DEOH p. 5-9).

Agency Requirements (DEOH pp. 5-9 to 5-10)

  1. Establish a category rating policy
  2. Define each quality category through job analysis per Uniform Guidelines
  3. Describe categories in the JOA + comply with RPL/CTAP/ICTAP
  4. Place applicants into categories based on assessed competencies, then apply veterans' preference
  5. Establish documentation/recordkeeping

Minimum: two categories. May NOT establish a "Not Qualified" category. Agencies are not required to disclose crediting plans/scoring keys (for test security), but must describe the categories in the JOA (DEOH p. 5-9).

How to Define Quality Categories — Three-Step Process (DEOH pp. 5-11 to 5-13)

Step 1: Identify job-related competencies/KSAs through job analysis
Step 2: Identify indicators of proficiency for each competency
Step 3: Identify level of proficiency required by position level, using one of two methods:

Method A — Possession Only: Categories defined by which competencies the applicant demonstrates.
Example: Best Qualified = demonstrates Oral Communication + Technical Knowledge + Project Management; Qualified = demonstrates Technical Knowledge only.

Method B — Specific Level: Categories defined by proficiency level within each competency.
Example: Best Qualified = Level 5 (complex) across all competencies; Well Qualified = Level 3 (moderate); Qualified = Level 1 (basic).
📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.5 Section B, pp. 5-9 to 5-13. Category rating authority (5 U.S.C. § 3319) is on p. 5-9. The requirement for a minimum of two quality categories is on p. 5-9. The three illustrative category structures (2-category and 3-category examples) are on p. 5-11. The method for defining categories using competency proficiency levels is on pp. 5-11 to 5-13. Key: under category rating, preference eligibles do NOT receive points — they are listed ahead of non-preference eligibles within each category (p. 5-9).
✦ Knowledge Check 1
An agency establishes categories: Best Qualified, Qualified, and Not Qualified. Valid?
✓ C is correct. "You may not establish a 'not qualified' category." Only positive categories. (DEOH Ch.5 B, p. 5-9)
✦ Knowledge Check 2
What are the two methods for defining quality categories under the DEOH's three-step process?
✓ A is correct. The DEOH defines two methods: Possession Only (categories based on which competencies are demonstrated) and Specific Level (categories based on proficiency levels). Both start from the same job analysis. (DEOH Ch.5 B, pp. 5-12 to 5-13)

6. Veterans' Preference in Category Rating — The CP/CPS Highest Category Rule

▼
Numerical RatingCategory Rating
Preference points added (5 or 10)Points NOT added; preference eligibles listed ahead within categories
CP/CPS get 10 points like other 10-point categoriesCP/CPS must be placed in highest quality category (except scientific/professional GS-9+)
Preference is absolute within score orderPreference is absolute within each category

Exception — Scientific and Professional at GS-9+

CP/CPS veterans are placed where their assessment score supports — they do NOT float. Verify series in OPM's Handbook of Occupational Groups and Families or Appendix K. Misidentification = violation of veterans' preference law (DEOH pp. 5-9, 6-14).

⚠ Exam Trap: Only CP and CPS float. TP (5-point) does NOT float. XP (10-point non-compensable) does NOT float. Only veterans with a compensable service-connected disability of 10%+ float to the highest category.
📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.5 Section B, p. 5-9 (and also Ch.6 B, pp. 6-12 to 6-13). The CP/CPS float rule: qualified preference eligibles with 10%+ compensable disability move to the top of the highest quality category. The exception: for professional/scientific positions at GS-9 and above, CP/CPS eligibles remain in their assessed category (preference within category, not float to top). The professional/scientific exception is on Ch.6 B, p. 6-13, and the list of professional/scientific series is in Appendix K.
✦ Knowledge Check
A CPS veteran (30%) for a GS-7 Admin Assistant scores in "Qualified." Where must they be placed?
✓ A is correct. "Qualified preference eligibles who have a compensable service-connected disability of 10 percent or more must be listed in the highest quality category." GS-7 Admin Assistant is not scientific/professional at GS-9+. (DEOH Ch.5 B, p. 5-9)

7. Case Examining vs. Competitor Inventory & 10-Point Eligible Rules

▼
FeatureCase ExaminingCompetitor Inventory (Register)
ConceptAnnounce, fill, closeBuild list for current and future vacancies
AdvantagesCompetencies tailored; higher availabilitySpeed; applicant applies once
DisadvantagesTime delay; applicants reapply each timeDecreasing availability; continual maintenance
When to useTargeted, specific vacanciesFrequently filled positions with non-status candidates where it's not efficient to recruit each time

10-Point Eligible Special Handling (DEOH pp. 5-27 to 5-28)

Under 5 U.S.C. § 3305, a 10-point eligible may file at any time for any position to which a non-temporary appointment was made within the preceding three years, or for which a list currently exists or is about to be established.

If the eligible applies for a specific position, meets qualifications, is within reach, and the certificate hasn't been issued → must be referred on the certificate as soon as possible. If the certificate was already issued → the hiring manager has the option of requesting the name be added.

10-Point Eligible File Documentation (DEOH p. 5-28)

To ensure a complete audit trail: "you should document the case file to show that the 10-point file was checked, and then you should document the names of any preference eligibles whose applications were pulled from the file and to whom additional material was sent."

📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.5 Section C, pp. 5-26 to 5-29. Case examining (one-time vacancy, rated for specific position) vs. competitor inventory (standing register, ongoing vacancies) is on pp. 5-26 to 5-27. The 10-point preference eligible rule for competitor inventories (must be given consideration from the date application is received even after cut-off) is on p. 5-28. The requirement to keep competitor inventories current and procedures for termination are on p. 5-29. Also see Appendix J for detailed competitor inventory guidance.
✦ Knowledge Check
A 10-point eligible applies for a position filled 2 years ago. No JOA is open. Must the agency accept the application?
✓ C is correct. Under 5 U.S.C. § 3305 and 5 CFR 332.311, a 10-point eligible may file any time within the three-year window. (DEOH Ch.5 C, p. 5-27)

8. Documentation, Notice of Results & Applicant Appeal Rights

▼

Required Documentation File (DEOH p. 5-25)

  • Position descriptions for each grade announced
  • Job analysis results (tasks, competencies/KSAs, linkages), date, quality level definitions, selective/quality ranking factors
  • Actual rating procedure used (per 5 CFR part 300), including transmutation tables if applicable
  • OPM qualification standard reference (or copy of agency-specific standard)
  • Raters' initials and dates for each applicant
  • Supplemental application form (if any — OMB approval required)
  • Tie-breaking method if used (must be job-related criteria)
  • Identification of SMEs and HR professionals (name, title, series, grade)

Notice of Results — Required Content (DEOH pp. 5-30 to 5-31)

The notice of results tells applicants whether they are qualified. It must include:

  • Title, series, grade, occupational specialty, JOA number or other identifier
  • Whether the applicant is eligible or ineligible
  • Level of veterans' preference awarded (if eligible)
  • Any restrictions on eligibility (e.g., "subject to meeting education requirements")
  • Whether the eligible was referred to the hiring manager
  • Brief explanation of the reason for not qualifying (e.g., "did not pass the written test," "did not meet basic experience requirements," "did not meet a mandatory selective factor")
  • Length of eligibility and extension procedures (competitor inventories only)
  • Point of contact
⚠ Exam Trap — Rating/Category Is Optional: The assigned numerical rating or quality category is optional in the notice. However, "this information should be made available to applicants upon request, if it exists" (DEOH p. 5-31). The exam may ask whether the notice must include the applicant's score — the answer is no, but it must be provided if the applicant asks.

Applicant Appeal of Rating (DEOH p. 5-31)

  1. Request in writing, stating why the original decision was improper
  2. Applicant must be advised the result could be higher, lower, or same
  3. First level: same office, different person than original rater
  4. Second level: designated agency official — decision is final
  5. Applicant cannot appeal further to OPM

Rating Change → Possible Priority Consideration

"Priority consideration may be required if the reconsideration process indicates the error resulted in the applicant's loss of bona fide consideration" (DEOH p. 5-31). This means a rating appeal that reveals an error leading to lost consideration triggers the priority consideration process from Chapter 6, Section E (see Unit 9).

When to Amend a Certificate After Rating Change

Once issued, do not amend unless: (1) new rating is "ineligible," (2) eligible was improperly awarded higher veterans' preference, or (3) error was by the rater/examining office (DEOH p. 5-31).

📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.5 Section B, p. 5-25 for the documentation file requirements (position descriptions, job analysis results, rating procedures, transmutation tables, rater initials, SME identification, etc.). The Notice of Results requirements are on Ch.5 Section C, pp. 5-30 to 5-31 (what to include: title/series/grade, eligible/ineligible status, numerical rating if rule-of-three, reconsideration rights, VP information). The applicant reconsideration (appeal) right under 5 CFR § 300.104 is on p. 5-31.
✦ Knowledge Check 1
An applicant requests their quality category placement. Is the agency required to provide this information?
✓ B is correct. The assigned rating or quality category is optional in the notice, "however, this information should be made available to applicants upon request, if it exists." (DEOH Ch.5 C, p. 5-31)
✦ Knowledge Check 2
An appeal results in a lower score. The applicant wants to appeal further to OPM. Is this possible?
✓ C is correct. "That decision is final. The applicant cannot appeal further to OPM." Applies to all applicants. (DEOH Ch.5 C, p. 5-31)

1. When Is an Applicant Entitled to Veterans' Preference?

▼

Veterans' preference is not just a policy — it is a statutory right rooted in Federal law. Understanding when it applies (and when it doesn't) is fundamental to the exam.

Where Preference Applies

  • Permanent and temporary positions in the competitive service — applied by both OPM and agencies under delegated examining authority
  • Most excepted service jobs in the executive branch (5 U.S.C. § 3320; 5 CFR part 302)

Basic Eligibility Requirement

To receive preference, a veteran must have been discharged or released from active duty under honorable conditions (with an honorable or general discharge). "Armed Forces" means the Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, and Coast Guard (5 U.S.C. § 2101(2)) (DEOH p. 4-17).

Key Restrictions

  • Military retirees at major/lieutenant commander or higher: NOT eligible for preference unless they are disabled veterans. Exception: Reservists who won't draw retired pay until age 60 are not affected by this restriction (DEOH p. 4-18).
  • Active duty for training (non-disabled): National Guard or Reserve training does NOT qualify as "active duty" for preference purposes for non-disabled veterans
  • Active duty for training (disabled): DOES count for disabled veterans, per MSPB decision in Hesse v. Department of the Army (DEOH p. 4-18)
⚠ Exam Trap — Active Duty for Training: The exam tests the asymmetry between disabled and non-disabled veterans regarding training service. For a non-disabled veteran, active duty for training in the Guard/Reserves does NOT qualify. For a disabled veteran, it DOES qualify. This distinction comes from the Hesse MSPB decision.
📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.4 Section B, pp. 4-17 to 4-18 — look for the “Veterans’ Preference” heading. The basic entitlement criteria (honorable discharge, active duty during qualifying period, campaign badge/expeditionary medal, or service-connected disability) are on pp. 4-17 to 4-18. The key threshold question — “did the veteran serve during a qualifying war, campaign, or expedition?” — leads you to VetGuide Appendix A for the specific list of qualifying periods.
✦ Knowledge Check 1
A retired Army Colonel (O-6) with no service-connected disability applies for a competitive service position. Is the Colonel entitled to veterans' preference?
✓ C is correct. "Military retirees at the rank of major, lieutenant commander, or higher are not eligible for preference in appointment unless they are disabled veterans." A Colonel is above the major threshold and has no disability. (DEOH Ch.4 B, p. 4-18)
✦ Knowledge Check 2
A National Guard member served 6 years of active duty for training only (no deployment, no disability). Is this service qualifying for veterans' preference?
✓ A is correct. "For non-disabled veterans, active duty for training in the National Guard or Reserves does not qualify as 'active duty' for preference." Only disabled veterans may credit training service. (DEOH Ch.4 B, p. 4-18)

2. Types of Veterans' Preference — TP, CP, CPS, XP, SSP, NV

▼

The DEOH defines six preference codes based on 5 U.S.C. §§ 2108 and 3309, as modified by length-of-service requirements in 38 U.S.C. § 5303A(d). Memorize these — the exam expects you to match the code to the definition quickly.

Veterans' Preference Categories

CodePointsDefinition
CPS10Service-connected disability of 30% or more. This triggers the strongest protections (OPM must approve pass-overs).
CP10Service-connected disability of 10% or more but less than 30%. Under category rating, both CP and CPS float to highest category (non-scientific/professional below GS-9).
XP10Two distinct groups: (1) Disability: Purple Heart recipients, non-compensable disability (<10%), or veterans receiving VA compensation/pension who don't qualify as CP/CPS. (2) Derived preference: Widow/widower, spouse, or parent of a deceased or disabled veteran (see Topic 3).
TP5Service during a war, during April 28, 1952–July 1, 1955, more than 180 consecutive days (other than training) in qualifying periods, during the Gulf War (Aug 2, 1990–Jan 2, 1992), or in a campaign/expedition for which a campaign medal was authorized.
SSP0Veterans released after August 29, 2008, by reason of a "sole survivorship discharge" who would otherwise have been eligible for 5-point preference if service had not been interrupted.
NV—Non-veteran (optional code; a blank space may also designate non-veterans).

Key TP Qualifying Periods (DEOH p. 4-19)

  • During a war (last qualifying war for preference = WWII: Dec 7, 1941 – Apr 28, 1952)
  • April 28, 1952 through July 1, 1955
  • More than 180 consecutive days (other than training), any part after Jan 31, 1955 and before Oct 15, 1976
  • Gulf War: August 2, 1990 through January 2, 1992
  • More than 180 consecutive days (other than training), any part during Sept 11, 2001 – Aug 31, 2010
  • In a campaign or expedition for which a campaign medal has been authorized

24-Month Continuous Service Requirement

Campaign medal holders or Gulf War veterans who originally enlisted after September 7, 1980 (or began active duty on or after October 14, 1982) and have not previously completed 24 months of continuous active duty must have served continuously for 24 months or the full period called/ordered (DEOH p. 4-20).

📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.4 Section B, pp. 4-18 to 4-20. The preference types and their point values under rule of three (TP=5, CP/CPS/XP=10) are on pp. 4-18 to 4-19. The military retiree restriction (major/LtCdr or above, no preference unless disabled) is on p. 4-18. The “sole survivorship” preference (0 points but listed ahead of non-preference eligibles) is on p. 4-20. Remember: under category rating, no points are assigned — preference is applied by listing ahead within categories.
✦ Knowledge Check 1
A veteran has a service-connected disability rated at 20% by the VA. Which preference code applies?
✓ B is correct. CP is for compensable disability of 10% or more but less than 30%. CPS requires 30% or more. At 20%, the veteran falls squarely in the CP range. (DEOH Ch.4 B, p. 4-18)
✦ Knowledge Check 2
A veteran received only the Global War on Terrorism Service Medal for service from 2005–2009 (no deployment, no combat, no disability). Is this veteran entitled to preference?
✓ C is correct. The VetGuide lists the "Global War on Terrorism Service Medal" specifically among "non-combat operations that are not qualifying for Veterans preference." However, the veteran may still qualify based on the length-of-service provisions (180+ consecutive days during Sept 11, 2001 – Aug 31, 2010) if other criteria are met. The medal itself is not qualifying. (VetGuide Appendix A)

3. Derived Preference (XP) — Spouse, Widow/Widower, and Parent Eligibility

▼

Derived preference (coded XP, 10 points) extends veterans' preference to certain family members of veterans. The rules are specific and the exam tests the conditions carefully.

Three Categories of Derived Preference (DEOH pp. 4-18 to 4-19)

1. Spouse of a Disabled Veteran

The spouse receives XP preference when the disabled veteran is disqualified for Federal employment along the general lines of their usual occupation because of a service-connected disability. This disqualification may be presumed when the veteran is unemployed AND:

  • Is rated by military/VA authorities as 100% disabled and/or unemployable, OR
  • Has retired, been separated, or resigned from a civil service position based on a service-connected disability, OR
  • Has attempted to obtain a civil service position and failed to qualify because of the disability

2. Widow/Widower of a Veteran

The widow or widower receives XP preference if they were not divorced from the veteran, have not remarried (or the remarriage was annulled), and the veteran either:

  • Served during a war, during April 28, 1952 – July 1, 1955, or in a campaign/expedition for which a campaign medal was authorized, OR
  • Died on active duty during qualifying service under conditions that would not have resulted in other than an honorable/general discharge

3. Parent of a Veteran

A parent receives XP preference in two situations:

  • Parent of deceased veteran: Veteran died under honorable conditions during a war, the 1952–1955 period, or campaign/expedition
  • Parent of disabled veteran: Veteran separated with honorable/general discharge and is permanently and totally disabled from service-connected injury/illness

In both cases, the parent must be: (a) the spouse of that parent is totally and permanently disabled, OR (b) the parent is unmarried or legally separated.

⚠ Exam Trap — No Derived Preference for Certain Deceased Veterans: "Preference is not given to widows/widowers or parents of deceased veterans who qualify for preference under 5 U.S.C. 2108(1)(B), (C), (D), or (2)." In plain language: the widow/widower or parent of a deceased disabled veteran who served after 1955 but did NOT serve in a war, campaign, or expedition is not entitled to derived preference. (DEOH p. 4-19)
📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.4 Section B, pp. 4-18 to 4-19. Derived preference (XP, 10 points) eligibility for spouse, widow/widower, and parent is on pp. 4-18 to 4-19. Key conditions: the veteran must be unable to work due to a service-connected disability, or the veteran is deceased/missing. Spousal derived preference based on common-law marriage claims must be referred to OPM’s General Counsel. Parent preference has additional income-based criteria.
✦ Knowledge Check
A veteran's spouse applies for a Federal position and claims derived XP preference. The veteran has a 100% VA disability rating and is unemployed. Is the spouse entitled to derived preference?
✓ B is correct. The spouse of a disabled veteran receives XP (10-point) derived preference when the veteran is disqualified for Federal employment due to service-connected disability. Disqualification "may be presumed when the veteran is unemployed" and "is rated by appropriate military or Department of Veterans Affairs authorities to be 100 percent disabled and/or unemployable." (DEOH Ch.4 B, p. 4-18)

4. Documentation — DD-214, SF-15, and Active Duty Certification (VOW Act)

▼

Standard Documentation

  • 5-point (TP): DD Form 214 (Certificate of Release or Discharge from Active Duty) showing character of discharge and dates of military service
  • 10-point (CP, CPS, XP): Standard Form 15 (Application for 10-Point Veterans' Preference) plus supporting documentation (VA letter, disability documentation, etc.)

Missing SF-15 Rule: "If an applicant submits the proof required to claim 10-point preference but is missing the SF 15, you should grant preference based on the documentation provided." (DEOH p. 4-18)

VOW Act — Active Duty Service Members

The VOW (Veterans Opportunity to Work) to Hire Heroes Act of 2011 requires agencies to treat certain active duty service members as preference eligibles even though they have not yet been discharged (DEOH p. 4-9).

Service members may submit a "certification" — any written document from the armed forces certifying they are expected to be discharged or released under honorable conditions not later than 120 days after submission (DEOH p. 4-9).

How to Adjudicate Active Duty Claims

Claimed PreferenceRequired DocumentationAction
0-point (SSP)Certification of sole survivorship discharge within 120 days + qualifying serviceGrant tentative 0-point preference
5-point (TP)Certification of honorable discharge within 120 days + qualifying serviceGrant tentative 5-point preference
10-pointSF-15 + disability documentation from military branch or VAGrant tentative 5- or 10-point based on documentation
10-point (Purple Heart)Official citation/document showing Purple Heart awardGrant 10-point preference
⚠ Exam Trap: The 120-day certification rule is the key detail. An active duty member can claim preference with a certification showing expected discharge within 120 days — but the preference is tentative until actual discharge documents (DD-214) are received. Proof of honorable separation is required before the actual appointment.
📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.4 Section A, pp. 4-9 to 4-11 for application documentation rules and Ch.4 B, p. 4-18 for SF-15 requirements. The DD-214 (Certificate of Release or Discharge) is the primary proof document. The SF-15 (Application for 10-Point Preference) is required for 10-point claims. Key rule: if the SF-15 is missing but other proof of preference is present, grant preference based on available documentation (p. 4-18). The VOW Act (Veterans Opportunity to Work) active duty certification for transitioning service members is on p. 4-11.
✦ Knowledge Check
An active duty service member applies claiming 10-point preference and submits VA disability documentation and a service certification, but no SF-15. Should the agency deny preference?
✓ A is correct. "If an applicant submits the proof required to claim 10-point preference but is missing the SF 15, you should grant preference based on the documentation provided." The SF-15 is the standard form, but the substantive documentation (VA letter, disability certification) is what matters. (DEOH Ch.4 B, p. 4-18)

5. Special Rules — Training Service, Military Retirees & Sole Survivorship

▼

Three Special Rules Summary

RuleDetails
Training Service (Non-Disabled)Active duty for training in Guard/Reserves does NOT qualify as "active duty" for preference. Only disabled veterans may credit training service.
Military Retiree RestrictionRetirees at major/lieutenant commander or above: no preference unless disabled veteran. Reservists not drawing retired pay until age 60 are exempt from this restriction.
Sole Survivorship Preference (SSP)0-point preference for veterans discharged after August 29, 2008, by reason of sole survivorship discharge who would otherwise have qualified for 5-point preference.

Why SSP Is 0 Points

SSP exists to ensure that veterans whose service was cut short by a sole survivorship discharge are not penalized — they receive the same access and protections as preference eligibles (such as late filing rights), even though they don't receive additional points.

📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.4 Section B, pp. 4-18 and 4-20. The military retiree restriction is on p. 4-18: retirees at the rank of major (O-4)/lieutenant commander or above receive no preference unless they have a service-connected disability. Training service (e.g., 6-month reservist training) does not qualify for preference — active duty for training alone is excluded. Sole survivorship preference (SSP, 0 points) is on p. 4-20.
✦ Knowledge Check
A Reserve Lieutenant Colonel (O-5) retires in 2020 at age 50 but will not draw military retired pay until age 60. Is this retiree subject to the preference restriction for retirees at major and above?
✓ C is correct. The DEOH explicitly exempts "Reservists who will not begin drawing military retired pay until age 60" from the restriction on retirees at major/lieutenant commander or higher. (DEOH Ch.4 B, p. 4-18)

6. VetGuide Appendix A — Qualifying Wars, Campaigns & Non-Qualifying Medals

▼

Critical Definitions from VetGuide

OPM considers "wars" only those armed conflicts for which a declaration of war was issued by Congress. The title 38 definition of "period of war" (which includes the Vietnam Era) is NOT applicable for civil service purposes (VetGuide Appendix A).

The last "war" for preference purposes is World War II: December 7, 1941 through April 28, 1952.

Non-Qualifying Medals

The following medals are specifically listed as NOT qualifying for preference:

  • Global War on Terrorism Service Medal
  • Medal of Merit (WWII meritorious service)
  • Medal of Freedom
  • Antarctica Service Medal
  • National Defense Service Medal
  • Armed Forces Service Medal
  • Armed Forces Reserve Medal

Qualifying Medals

"Any Armed Forces Expeditionary Medal, whether listed [in the VetGuide] or not, is qualifying for Veterans preference." Campaign medals such as those for El Salvador, Lebanon, Grenada, Panama, Southwest Asia, Somalia, and Haiti all qualify (DEOH p. 4-20).

⚠ Exam Trap — GWOT Service Medal vs. Expeditionary Medal: The Global War on Terrorism Service Medal is NOT qualifying. But the Global War on Terrorism Expeditionary Medal IS qualifying (as it is a campaign/expeditionary medal). The exam may present a veteran claiming preference based solely on the GWOT Service Medal — this is not a valid basis.
📖 On the exam, VetGuide Appendix A is one of the five reference documents available. It lists every qualifying war, campaign, and expedition with specific date ranges. It also lists non-qualifying medals (e.g., Armed Forces Expeditionary Medal for certain operations does not qualify if the dates fall outside qualifying periods). Use this document to verify whether a veteran’s service period falls within a qualifying campaign. Also see DEOH Ch.4 B, p. 4-20 for context on how campaign badges and expeditionary medals establish preference entitlement.
✦ Knowledge Check
For civil service veterans' preference purposes, what is the last "war" that qualifies?
✓ B is correct. "OPM considers to be 'wars' only those armed conflicts for which a declaration of war was issued by Congress." The title 38 definition including the Vietnam Era "is NOT applicable for civil service purposes." The last congressionally declared war was WWII. (VetGuide Appendix A)

7. When Proof of Preference Is Required & Positions Restricted to Preference Eligibles

▼

Three Situations Requiring Proof (DEOH p. 4-20)

  1. When preference status is used as a basis for accepting an application after the closing date
  2. When positions are restricted to preference eligibles (custodian, elevator operator, guard, and messenger — 5 U.S.C. § 3310)
  3. Prior to final selection if the preference eligible was selected over other eligibles based on entitlement to veterans' preference

Positions Restricted to Preference Eligibles

Four position types are restricted by statute (5 U.S.C. § 3310): custodian, elevator operator, guard, and messenger. These positions may only be filled by preference eligibles as long as qualified preference eligibles are available (DEOH p. 4-20).

📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.4 Section B, p. 4-20. When proof of preference is required: before certification (if possible) or at time of appointment. Positions restricted by law to preference eligibles (5 U.S.C. § 3310) include: custodian, elevator operator, guard, and messenger positions. These restricted positions are also discussed in Ch.6 A. If no preference eligible is available for a restricted position, the position cannot be filled by a non-preference eligible without OPM approval.
✦ Knowledge Check
Which of the following positions is restricted by statute to preference eligibles?
✓ C is correct. Under 5 U.S.C. § 3310, four positions are restricted to preference eligibles: custodian, elevator operator, guard, and messenger. Guard is one of the four. (DEOH Ch.4 B, p. 4-20)

8. Applying Veterans' Preference — Rule of Three vs. Category Rating

▼

This topic ties together the preference rules from this unit with the rating procedures from Unit 6. The exam tests your ability to apply preference correctly under each system.

Side-by-Side Application

FeatureRule of Three (Numerical)Category Rating
How points work5 or 10 points added to numeric scorePoints NOT added; preference applied within categories
Ordering within listBy augmented numerical score (highest to lowest)Preference eligibles listed ahead of non-preference within each category
CP/CPS special rulePoints added like other preference categoriesMust be placed in highest quality category (exception: scientific/professional GS-9+)
SelectionFrom top 3 eligibles on certificateFrom highest quality category with eligibles
Passing over preference eligibleRequires justification; CPS requires OPM approvalSame protections apply within categories
Worked Example — Category Rating:

Position: GS-7 Management Analyst. Three quality categories: Best Qualified, Well Qualified, Qualified.

Applicant pool after assessment:
• Adams (non-veteran) — Best Qualified
• Baker (TP, 5-point) — Well Qualified
• Chen (CPS, 30% disability) — assessed score places in Well Qualified
• Davis (non-veteran) — Best Qualified

After applying preference:
Best Qualified: Chen (CPS — floated to highest category), Adams, Davis
Well Qualified: Baker (TP — listed ahead of non-veterans)

Chen floats to Best Qualified even though the assessment score was Well Qualified, because CPS veterans with 10%+ compensable disability must be in the highest category. Within Best Qualified, Chen is listed first (preference eligible before non-preference). The hiring manager selects from Best Qualified.
⚠ Exam Trap: Under category rating, the hiring manager may select any applicant in the highest quality category — unlike rule of three where selection is limited to the top 3. This means a non-preference eligible in the Best Qualified category can be selected over a preference eligible in the same category. However, if a preference eligible is passed over, the pass-over rules still apply (see Units 9–10).
📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.5 Section B, pp. 5-8 to 5-9 for how VP applies in both systems. Under rule of three: add 5 or 10 points to earned score, then rank numerically (p. 5-8). Under category rating: no points added; preference eligibles listed ahead of non-preference eligibles within each quality category; CP/CPS float to top of highest category except for professional/scientific at GS-9+ (p. 5-9). Also see Ch.4 B, pp. 4-18 to 4-20 for the preference type definitions and point values.
✦ Knowledge Check
Under category rating for a GS-5 Administrative Assistant position, a CP veteran (15% disability) scores in the "Qualified" range. Where must this veteran be placed?
✓ A is correct. Under category rating, "qualified preference eligibles who have a compensable service-connected disability of 10 percent or more must be listed in the highest quality category." CP (15% disability) meets the 10% threshold. This is a GS-5 non-scientific/professional position, so the exception doesn't apply. Both CP and CPS float to the top — not just CPS. (DEOH Ch.5 B, p. 5-9)

1. Geographic Area of Consideration, Residency & Gender Restrictions

▼

Basic Residency Rule

In competitive examining, qualified and available applicants must be considered for employment referral regardless of their place of residence. Residency is a non-merit factor prohibited by 5 CFR § 300.103(c) (DEOH p. 6-2).

The Single Exception

Residency may be required only when established by statute. These cases are "very rare." Example: Department of Labor VETS State Director positions (DEOH p. 6-2).

Positions Restricted to One Gender (DEOH p. 6-10)

An appointing officer is generally prohibited from restricting employment to one gender (5 CFR 332.407). OPM may authorize exceptions in unusual circumstances. The agency must submit a written explanation before the job is announced to Employee Services, 1900 E Street NW, Room 6500, Washington, DC 20415. The justification must show that single gender is a bona fide occupational qualification — demonstrating the relationship between gender and the position, and the impact on performance.

⚠ Exam Trap: Gender restrictions require OPM approval before announcement. If the exam presents a scenario where an agency restricts to one gender after announcing, that's wrong. Also, note this is the same OPM address used for qualifications-based pass-overs — Employee Services at 1900 E Street.
📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.6 Section A, pp. 6-2 and 6-10. Residency cannot be used as a rating/ranking factor unless required by statute (5 CFR § 300.103(c)) — this is on p. 6-10. Gender restrictions: guard and messenger positions are restricted to preference eligibles, and custodian/elevator operator positions are similarly restricted (p. 6-2). The general rule for area of consideration in DE is “all U.S. citizens” — agencies cannot limit to a geographic area unless using merit promotion or a separate authority. Also see 5 CFR § 332.407.
✦ Knowledge Check
A hiring manager wants to restrict a position to one gender but has already posted the JOA. Can the gender restriction be applied now?
✓ A is correct. "You must submit a written explanation, before the job is announced, as to why such a restriction is warranted." OPM must determine the single gender is a bona fide occupational qualification. The timing matters — it must be done before announcement. (DEOH Ch.6 A, p. 6-10)

2. Interdisciplinary Positions — Classification, Certification & the One-Certificate Rule

▼

Definition and Key Constraints

  • Duties closely related to more than one professional or scientific occupation
  • Only professional/scientific occupations may be cited — no administrative, technical, or clerical (DEOH p. 6-3)
  • Persons in any of the relevant professions may be considered equally well-qualified

Two Categories

Category 1Category 2
Specific combination of competencies from two+ series — some duties from one, other duties from anotherCompetencies characteristic of either of two+ series — work is substantially identical
Ex: Nuclear disposal project — civil + nuclear engineeringEx: Flood control research — civil engineering or hydrology

The One-Certificate Rule

Issue only one Certificate of Eligibles. All applicants rated on the same competencies/KSAs. The series for each eligible is noted next to their name. The qualifications of the person selected determines the final classification (DEOH p. 6-4).

📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.6 Section A, pp. 6-3 to 6-4. The two categories of interdisciplinary positions are on p. 6-3 (Category 1: combination of competencies from two professions; Category 2: work characteristic of either profession). The one-certificate rule is on p. 6-4: you must create and issue only one Certificate of Eligibles for a single interdisciplinary position. All applicants rated on the same competencies regardless of series. The list of professional/scientific series is in Appendix K.
✦ Knowledge Check
For an interdisciplinary GS-810/GS-1315 position, an HR specialist prepares two separate certificates. Is this correct?
✓ B is correct. "You must create and issue only one Certificate of Eligibles for a single interdisciplinary position." (DEOH Ch.6 A, p. 6-4)

3. Dual Certification — Multiple Grades, Specialties & Locations

▼

Two Methods (5 CFR 332.402)

Method 1: Single CertificationMethod 2: Dual Certification
Refer on one certificate at a time. Temporarily removes from all other consideration. Must notify candidates. Refer simultaneously on all certificates for which eligible, interested, and within reach. No limit.

Mandatory dual certification: For a single job vacancy at multiple grade levels, or if there is a shortage of well-qualified eligibles (DEOH p. 6-5). Single certification "must be an exception to the examining procedures, not the normal operation."

Quick Reference (DEOH p. 6-7)

SituationRule
Multiple gradesCertify from lowest grade willing to accept to highest qualified, if within reach
Multiple specialtiesCertify only for specialties qualified for and within reach
Multiple locationsCertify to each location selected by applicant, if within reach
📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.6 Section A, pp. 6-5 to 6-7. The two alternative methods of certifying eligibles (5 CFR 332.402) are on p. 6-5: (1) refer on only one certificate at a time (temporarily removing from other consideration), or (2) dual certification — refer simultaneously on all certificates for which the eligible qualifies. Under dual certification, there is no limit to the number of certificates on which a candidate can appear. Candidate notification requirements differ by method — check p. 6-5 for the notification language required under each option.
✦ Knowledge Check
A JOA is open for one vacancy at GS-7/9/11. An applicant qualifies at GS-7 and GS-9 but not GS-11, and will accept GS-7. How many certificates?
✓ C is correct. "You must certify an eligible for all positions, from the lowest grade or salary he or she is willing to accept to the highest-grade level for which he or she is qualified and within reach." (DEOH Ch.6 A, p. 6-6)

4. Creating a Certificate of Eligibles Under Category Rating

▼

Order of Eligibles — Standard (Most Positions) (DEOH p. 6-12)

OrderEntitlementDetails
1ICTAP eligibles5 CFR part 330 — well-qualified displaced employees from other agencies
2Priority considerationEligibles who lost consideration due to erroneous certification/illegal selection
3Highest quality categoryIncluding CP/CPS floated to top (except scientific/professional GS-9+). Preference eligibles listed ahead of non-preference.

Order of Eligibles — Restricted Positions (DEOH p. 6-14)

For custodian, elevator operator, guard, and messenger positions, the ordering is expanded to seven levels:

OrderEntitlement
1ICTAP eligibles entitled to veterans' preference
2Preference eligibles who lost consideration due to erroneous certification
3All 10-point preference eligibles with 10%+ service-connected disability AND preference eligibles in highest quality category
4All remaining preference eligibles in quality category order
5Non-preference ICTAP eligibles
6Non-preference eligibles who lost consideration
7All remaining non-preference eligibles (only if non-veterans were allowed to compete)

Preference-Only Certification Option

"If allowable under your agency's category rating policy, you may certify only all of the preference eligibles in the highest quality category when there are a sufficient number to consider to fill the vacant position(s)." (DEOH p. 6-16) This lets agencies limit certification to preference eligibles when enough are available.

Unrated Eligibles (DEOH p. 6-14)

When there are three or fewer eligibles and they are either all preference or all non-preference, you do not need to rate and rank. List them randomly with a notation "Eligible" in lieu of a rating. However, if there are more than three, or if the group is a mix of preference and non-preference, you must use category rating procedures.

Required Items on a Certificate (DEOH p. 6-17)

Certificate number, title/series/grade, duty location, eligibles' names, veterans' preference symbol (CPS, CP, XP, TP, SSP, or NV), signature of issuing officer, issue date, return/due date.

Number of Names Certified

Certify all eligible candidates in the highest quality category. If fewer than three, may merge with the next lower category (DEOH p. 6-16).

Certificate Expiration

OPM does not dictate a specific period. Generally valid until stated vacancies are filled. Most agencies set 30- or 45-day initial period. When vacancies are stated as "few" or "many," re-announcing after 9 months to 1 year may be warranted to clear ICTAP (DEOH p. 6-18).

⚠ Exam Trap — Rule of Three Does NOT Apply: "The 'three consideration' rule embodied in 5 CFR 332.405 does not apply in category rating." The hiring manager may select any eligible in the highest quality category. The "three" only matters for merging: fewer than three triggers the merge option. (DEOH p. 6-23)
📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.6 Section B, pp. 6-11 to 6-18 — titled “Creating a Certificate of Eligibles.” The category rating certification order (ICTAP → lost-consideration priority → highest quality category with preference eligibles ahead) is on pp. 6-12 to 6-13. The CP/CPS float rule is on p. 6-12. The certificate validity period and the requirement that names are listed in any order within a category (subject to preference) are on p. 6-11. The rule that no numeric scores are assigned under category rating is on p. 6-12.
✦ Knowledge Check 1
A certificate for a guard position (restricted to preference eligibles) has both preference and non-preference ICTAP eligibles. In what order are they listed?
✓ A is correct. For restricted positions, the 7-level ordering separates ICTAP eligibles by preference status. Preference ICTAP eligibles are at Order 1; non-preference ICTAP eligibles are at Order 5, below all preference eligibles. (DEOH Ch.6 B, p. 6-14)
✦ Knowledge Check 2
There are only 2 eligibles for a position and both are non-preference. Must the HR specialist assign quality category ratings?
✓ C is correct. "When there are three or fewer eligibles and they are either all preference eligibles or all nonpreference eligibles, you do not need to rate and rank the eligibles. You may simply list the eligibles randomly on the certificate with a notation 'Eligible' in lieu of a rating." (DEOH Ch.6 B, p. 6-14)

5. Professional & Scientific Positions at GS-9+ — The CP/CPS Exception

▼
Most PositionsProfessional/Scientific at GS-9+
CP/CPS must be placed in highest quality categoryCP/CPS placed in category their assessment score supports — no float
Preference is absolute within each categoryPreference is still absolute within each category

Verify series using OPM's Handbook of Occupational Groups and Families or Appendix K. Misidentification could violate veterans' preference law and invalidate an appointment (DEOH p. 6-14).

⚠ Exam Trap: Same CPS veteran, different outcome based on position: GS-7 Admin Assistant → floats to Best Qualified. GS-11 Research Biologist (professional/scientific) → stays where score places them.
📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.6 Section B, pp. 6-13 to 6-14. The professional/scientific exception is under “Professional and Scientific Positions at GS-09 and Above” on p. 6-13: CP/CPS eligibles are NOT automatically placed at the top of the highest category. Instead, they remain in their assessed category with preference within that category (5 U.S.C. § 3313). The ranking order for these positions is on p. 6-13. The list of qualifying professional/scientific series is in Appendix K — bookmark this appendix for exam day.
✦ Knowledge Check
A CPS veteran applies for a GS-12 Wildlife Biologist (professional) and scores in Well Qualified. A non-preference eligible scores in Best Qualified. Who ranks higher?
✓ B is correct. "For professional or scientific positions at grades GS-9 and above, compensably disabled preference eligibles (CPS/CP)...are not automatically placed at the top." (DEOH Ch.6 B, p. 6-13)

6. Supplemental Certification, Merging Categories & Amending Certificates

▼

Supplemental Certificate — Six Matching Conditions (DEOH p. 6-19)

A certificate is a supplement (not a new cert) only when these six items are the same as the original:

  1. Appointing Officer
  2. Type of Appointment
  3. Duty Location
  4. Title, Series, Grade
  5. Qualification Factors (minimum qualifications, selective factors, competencies/KSAs, passing grade)
  6. Employment Conditions (travel, part-time vs. full-time, etc.)

Standing rule: Eligibles on a supplement have lower standing than the original. Even a 10-point preference late filer on a supplement has lower standing than non-preference eligibles on the original (DEOH p. 6-20).

Supplements may only be issued while the original is still outstanding (not yet audited). After audit, a new certificate is required.

Amending a Certificate (DEOH p. 6-18)

You may need to amend an already-issued certificate to:

  • Add a name inadvertently left off
  • Add legitimate late filers (see Ch.4)

Removing an applicant from a certificate without going through the objection/pass-over process would be appropriate only under "extremely rare circumstances" (DEOH p. 6-18). Document all amendments for third-party reconstruction.

Merging Categories — Two Decision Points (DEOH p. 6-20)

WhenWho DecidesKey Rule
Before issuingExamining office + hiring managerPreference eligibles from both categories rise to top of merged category
After working (fewer than 3 remain)Hiring manager + examining officeOnce merged, new order must be maintained
⚠ Exam Trap — The Merging Trap: Before merging: Best Qualified has 2 non-preference eligibles → manager selects freely. After merging: TP veteran from Well Qualified rises to top of merged category → must be selected first or pass-over required. "These potential outcomes should prompt a discussion with the hiring manager before making a decision to merge." (DEOH p. 6-21)
📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.6 Section B, pp. 6-18 to 6-22. Supplemental certification (for late filers) is on pp. 6-19 to 6-20 — key rule: eligibles on a supplement have lower standing than those on the original certificate. Merging categories is on pp. 6-20 to 6-22: if fewer than 3 candidates in the highest category, you may merge the top two categories. Once merged, the new order must be maintained and preference rules apply. Merging is optional, not mandatory. The number of merges is limited only by the number of categories established.
✦ Knowledge Check 1
After selections, only 2 eligibles remain in Best Qualified. The hiring manager wants more candidates. Can categories be merged?
✓ A is correct. Merging is available at two points. No OPM approval needed — it's an agency decision subject to the agency's category rating policy. (DEOH Ch.6 B, p. 6-20)
✦ Knowledge Check 2
A supplemental certificate lists a 10-point preference eligible. The original certificate still has unselected non-preference eligibles. Who has higher standing?
✓ C is correct. "Even a 10-point preference late filer in the highest category certified on a supplemental certificate has a lower standing than those nonpreference eligibles on the original certificate." (DEOH Ch.6 B, p. 6-20)

7. Selection Procedures & The Preference-First Rule

▼

The Core Selection Rule

A hiring manager may select any eligible in the highest quality category, EXCEPT: may not select a non-preference eligible over a preference eligible unless a pass-over is submitted and approved per 5 U.S.C. § 3318 (DEOH p. 6-23).

Decision Flowchart:
All preference eligibles in highest category? → Select any one freely.
All non-preference? → Select any one freely.
Mix? → Must select a preference eligible first, OR get pass-over approved.
📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.6 Section B, pp. 6-23 to 6-24. Selection from the highest quality category: the hiring manager may select any eligible in the category, but must select preference eligibles ahead of non-preference eligibles unless pass-over procedures are followed (5 U.S.C. § 3318). The “three consideration” rule (5 CFR 332.405) applies only under rule of three, NOT under category rating (p. 6-24). The requirement for the hiring manager to sign and date the returned certificate is on p. 6-24.
✦ Knowledge Check
Highest category has 3 preference and 4 non-preference eligibles. Manager wants the non-preference who interviewed best. What must happen?
✓ B is correct. "A hiring manager may not select a non-preference eligible over a preference eligible" without an approved pass-over per 5 U.S.C. § 3318. Interview performance does not override. (DEOH Ch.6 B, p. 6-23)

8. Reporting Codes, Certificate Audit & BWA Authority

▼

Reporting Codes (DEOH pp. 6-26 to 6-28)

CodeMeaningDocumentation / Key Rule
ASelectedAnnotate with date of selection for reconstruction
D(x)DeclinedDA=agency, DD=later date, DE=exam program, DG=grade, DL=location, DP=position, DX=further Federal, DZ=other. Written statement preferred. Oral declinations must be documented promptly. Oral declinations must come from the eligible — declinations from anyone other than the eligible are not acceptable.
FRFailure to RespondRetain documentation of contact attempt. Agency FR procedures must be applied uniformly and consistently.
CRUReturned UnclaimedRetain undelivered envelope (preferably unopened) or undeliverable email notification
CECurrently EmployedALL five conditions must match (see below)
TETemp/Time-LimitedPosition being filled is non-permanent; eligible in temp/time-limited appointment
NS/NNNot SelectedNS = contacted, not selected. NN = not contacted, not selected.
NCNon-Competitive AppointmentEligible was on cert but selected through VRA, 30% disabled, etc.
RM/RQ/RSRemovedRM = Medical (5 CFR 339). RQ = Qualifications (5 CFR 332). RS = Suitability (5 CFR 332).
⚠ Exam Trap — Oral Declinations: "Oral declinations must come from the eligible; declinations from anyone other than the eligible are not acceptable." (DEOH p. 6-26) If a spouse, roommate, or supervisor calls to decline on behalf of the eligible, that declination is NOT valid. The official must document who provided the declination.

FR for Interview No-Shows (DEOH p. 6-27)

An eligible who fails to appear for an interview may be coded FR only when both conditions are met:

  1. You sent written notice (mail or email) that failure to appear = removal from consideration (retain a copy)
  2. The interview arrangements were reasonable — consider the importance of the interview, travel required, and notice given

With telephone and video conferencing available, travel beyond the commuting area should normally not be required unless the job is above clerical level or above journeyman level in skilled trades.

CE Code — Five Conditions (DEOH p. 6-28)

  1. Same appointing officer
  2. Same type of position (full-time, part-time)
  3. Same or higher grade
  4. Same duty location
  5. Same type of appointment (permanent, temporary)

TE is used when the position being filled is non-permanent and the eligible is in a temporary/time-limited appointment. If even one CE condition doesn't match, CE does not apply.

BWA Authority Code

Authority code BWA must be used on SF-50s for DE appointments. Requires the agency's DE agreement number and certificate number (DEOH p. 6-32).

Two Audit Responsibilities (DEOH p. 6-25)

  1. All documentation for declinations and removals is in order
  2. All selections comply with applicable rules and veterans' preference was applied correctly

Audit before the selectee's first day — improper certificates may invalidate appointments with cascading effects.

Improper Removal from Consideration

If an eligible's name was removed improperly (e.g., reported as declined but no documentation), the code must be changed to "not selected" (NS or NN) before auditing begins (DEOH p. 6-29).

📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.6 Section C, pp. 6-25 to 6-32 — titled “Auditing a Certificate.” The two basic audit responsibilities are on p. 6-25: (1) verify all documentation for declinations/removals and (2) verify selections comply with applicable rules and VP. The critical reason to audit before entry on duty is on p. 6-25 (cascading effect of improper appointments). The BWA authority code for SF-50s is on p. 6-32. The SF-39 symbols for reporting actions on eligibles are on p. 6-24. The worked certificate example with dates and selection annotations is on pp. 6-29 to 6-31.
✦ Knowledge Check 1
An eligible's spouse calls the agency and says the eligible is no longer interested in the position. Can the agency code this as a declination (D)?
✓ A is correct. "Oral declinations must come from the eligible; declinations from anyone other than the eligible are not acceptable." The agency must contact the eligible directly to confirm. (DEOH Ch.6 C, p. 6-26)
✦ Knowledge Check 2
An eligible fails to appear for an interview. The agency coded them FR. However, no written notice was sent warning that failure to appear would result in removal. Is the FR valid?
✓ B is correct. An interview no-show may be coded FR only when: (1) written notice was sent that failure to appear = removal, AND (2) arrangements were reasonable. Without the written notice, the FR is not valid. (DEOH Ch.6 C, p. 6-27)

9. Shared Certificates (Competitive Service Act of 2015)

▼

The Competitive Service Act of 2015 (Public Law 114-137) allows agencies to share competitive certificates across departments and agencies. This is a newer provision with many specific rules the exam can test.

What Can Be Shared

A competitive certificate issued under delegated examining may be shared with one or more agencies for a position that matches on all four criteria (DEOH p. 6-51):

  1. Same occupational series
  2. Same grade level (or equivalent)
  3. Same full performance level
  4. Same duty location

May be filled on a permanent or term basis. All actions must be completed within 240 calendar days from the date the original agency issued the certificate.

Original Agency (Sharing) Requirements

  • Must include opt-in instructions in the original JOA (applicants choose whether to allow sharing)
  • Must audit the certificate first, including resolving all objections and pass-overs, before sharing
  • Must share in original form (preserve original ordering)
  • Must redact names of applicants who did not opt in, and names of those the original agency selected
  • Must safeguard PII during transmission
  • Must notify receiving agencies if an error is discovered

Receiving Agency Requirements

  • Must verify through job analysis that the original qualifications, selective factors, and competencies/KSAs are appropriate for its position
  • Must notify candidates of its receipt and intent to consider them
  • Must consider own employees and CTAP/RPL eligibles first — give them up to 10 business days to apply
  • Must provide ICTAP priority to ICTAP eligibles who applied to the original JOA
  • Cannot reassess applicants — must use the original ratings
  • Cannot re-share the certificate to another agency
  • Must audit the certificate upon completion

Objections and Pass-Overs on Shared Certificates

Critical rule: Adjudications by the original agency (or OPM for CPS pass-overs) do NOT extend to the receiving agency. Each receiving agency must adjudicate objections and pass-overs independently, on a case-by-case basis (DEOH p. 6-53).

⚠ Exam Trap — Three Key Prohibitions for Receiving Agencies:
1. Cannot reassess — must accept the original ratings as-is
2. Cannot re-share — the certificate stops with the receiving agency
3. Original agency's pass-over decisions do not carry over — the receiving agency must adjudicate its own objections/pass-overs independently
📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.6 Section F, pp. 6-51 to 6-53 — this is the Shared Certificates section (Competitive Service Act of 2015, Public Law 114-137). The 240-day rule is in the second paragraph: a shared certificate is valid for 240 days from the original issuance date. Key restrictions: the receiving agency cannot re-rate, re-rank, or re-assess applicants, and cannot re-share the certificate with a third agency. The receiving agency must use the same selection procedures as the originating agency. This is only 3 pages — bookmark it for exam day.
✦ Knowledge Check 1
Agency A shared a certificate with Agency B. Agency A had sustained an objection to an eligible and removed them (coded RQ). Must Agency B also consider this eligible removed?
✓ C is correct. "Adjudications by the original hiring agency (or OPM in the case of 30 percent or more disabled veterans) sustaining objections or granting passover requests do not extend to a receiving agency." Each receiving agency decides independently. (DEOH Ch.6 F, p. 6-53)
✦ Knowledge Check 2
A receiving agency wants to re-rank the applicants on a shared certificate using its own assessment tool. Is this permissible?
✓ B is correct. "A receiving agency may not reassess the applicants for purposes of rating and ranking." The receiving agency must use the original ratings. It must, however, verify through job analysis that the original qualifications and competencies are appropriate for its position. (DEOH Ch.6 F, p. 6-53)

1. Suitability vs. Qualifications — The Critical Distinction

▼

The DEOH draws a sharp line between suitability and qualifications — and the exam tests this distinction repeatedly because it determines who has authority to adjudicate and what procedures apply.

Definitions

SuitabilityQualifications
Refers to identifiable character traits and conduct sufficient to determine whether an individual can carry out duties "in such a manner as to protect the integrity or promote the efficiency of the service" (DEOH p. 6-33) Refers to a person's ability as measured by experience, education, and competencies/KSAs
Arises from the President's authority to "ascertain the fitness of applicants as to character" (5 U.S.C. § 3301) Based on OPM qualification standards, selective factors, and assessment results
Example: Misconduct, negligence in prior employment, false statements Example: Lacks required specialized experience or education

Key principle: A person may be well qualified but unsuitable — for example, someone with excellent experience who made material false statements on their application (DEOH p. 6-33).

Three Categories That Must Be Referred to OPM

Under 5 CFR § 731.103(d)(2), agencies must refer cases to OPM for a suitability determination when evidence arises of:

  1. Material, intentional false statement in examination or appointment
  2. Deception or fraud in examination or appointment
  3. Refusal to furnish testimony as required by 5 CFR § 5.4

The agency cannot adjudicate these on its own — they must go to OPM. All other suitability issues may be adjudicated by the agency (DEOH p. 6-33).

⚠ Exam Trap: The exam presents scenarios involving false statements and asks what the agency must do. The answer is always: refer to OPM for a suitability determination. The agency cannot handle material/intentional false statements internally. This rule also applies to the objection/pass-over process — fraud-based objections go to OPM because of the suitability nexus.
📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.6 Section C, p. 6-33. The suitability vs. qualifications distinction is critical: suitability (5 CFR part 731) concerns character/conduct; qualifications concern ability to perform the job. The three suitability categories that must be referred to OPM are listed here: material intentional false statement, deception/fraud in examination or appointment, and refusal to furnish testimony under 5 CFR § 5.4. Also see Ch.1 C, p. 1-7 for the pre-offer/post-offer suitability inquiry rules.
✦ Knowledge Check 1
During a background investigation, the agency discovers a tentative selectee made material, intentional false statements on their application. Who adjudicates?
✓ B is correct. Under 5 CFR § 731.103(d)(2), "cases involving evidence of material, intentional false statement in examination or appointment, deception or fraud in examination or appointment, or refusal to furnish testimony" must be referred to OPM. The agency cannot make this determination. (DEOH Ch.6 C, p. 6-33)
✦ Knowledge Check 2
An applicant has excellent qualifications but was terminated from their last Federal position for negligence. Is it possible for this applicant to be qualified but unsuitable?
✓ C is correct. Suitability and qualifications are distinct determinations. "A person who is well qualified for a position may nonetheless be deemed unsuitable for Federal employment due to factors listed in the suitability criteria, such as misconduct or negligence in prior employment." (DEOH Ch.6 C, p. 6-33)

2. Objecting to an Eligible — Definition, Grounds & The Four-Step Process

▼

Definition

An objection is an agency's request to remove an eligible from consideration on a particular certificate. When standing registers are used, an objection removes the eligible from the register and future certificates drawn from it (DEOH p. 6-35).

The "Proper and Adequate Reasons" Standard

An objection may be sustained only if based on proper and adequate reasons. The DEOH is explicit about what does NOT qualify: "It is not appropriate to attempt to eliminate an eligible on the grounds that one or more non-preference eligibles in the same quality category or one or more lower ranking eligibles are better qualified." (DEOH p. 6-35)

Grounds for Objection (DEOH pp. 6-35 to 6-37)

GroundKey Detail
AffiliationsConflict of interest that could clearly be expected
AgeGenerally NOT sufficient alone; valid only for minimum age or maximum entry-age positions
EducationLack of education is NOT valid unless education is an absolute minimum requirement (5 U.S.C. § 3308)
ExperienceValid only when the lacking experience is part of minimum requirements (general, specialized, or selective factors)
Fraud/False StatementsSubject to OPM approval for all applicants (not just preference eligibles) — must be sent to OPM for suitability determination
Habitual Alcohol UseValid without evidence of rehabilitation — must show inability to perform duties or threat to safety
Illegal Narcotics UseValid without evidence of substantial rehabilitation
MedicalPhysical/medical condition preventing safe, efficient performance of full duties. Preference eligible medical disqualification requires OPM approval (5 CFR § 339.306)
Personal CharacteristicsMust identify which job elements require the traits and demonstrate through specific examples how the eligible lacks them
Previous ServiceNegligence or misconduct in prior employment (same or another agency)
National SecurityIf position requires national security eligibility and applicant is ineligible — agency has legal responsibility; DEU need not know specific reasons

The Four-Step Objection Process (DEOH pp. 6-38 to 6-39)

Step 1 — Hiring Manager: Submits reasons for objecting to the examining office

Step 2 — Examining Office: Reviews and decides. If sustained → accepts reasons for removal. If not sustained → rejects reasons; eligible stays on certificate.

Step 3 — Examining Office: Notifies the hiring manager of the decision in writing

Step 4 — Hiring Manager: If sustained → removes eligible. If not sustained → three options: (1) seek reconsideration with additional information, (2) consider/select the eligible, or (3) decline to make a selection from the certificate.

Pending Objections and Continued Selections

While an objection is pending, the hiring manager may continue making additional selections as long as at least one vacancy remains unfilled in case the objection is not sustained (5 CFR § 332.406(e)) (DEOH p. 6-38).

No Applicant Appeal Right

Under 5 CFR § 332.406, concerning objections based on qualifications, "there is no statutory or regulatory right for applicants to appeal" (DEOH p. 6-35).

⚠ Exam Trap — "Better Qualified" Is Never a Valid Basis: The exam's most common objection scenario: a hiring manager wants to pass over a preference eligible because a non-preference eligible "performed better in the interview" or has "more relevant experience." This is attempting to eliminate an eligible on the grounds that others are better qualified — and the DEOH explicitly prohibits this. Comparative superiority is not a proper and adequate reason.
📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.6 Section D, pp. 6-35 to 6-39. The definition of an objection and the permissible grounds are on p. 6-35 (suitability reasons, medical reasons, lack of qualifications). The four-step objection process is on pp. 6-35 to 6-39, formatted as a step table. Key: an objection is a request to remove an eligible from the certificate; a pass-over is a request to skip a preference eligible and select a lower-ranked non-preference eligible. See 5 CFR § 332.406 for the regulatory basis.
✦ Knowledge Check 1
A hiring manager wants to object to a preference eligible because a non-preference eligible in the same category had a stronger interview. Is this a valid objection?
✓ C is correct. "It is not appropriate to attempt to eliminate an eligible on the grounds that one or more non-preference eligibles in the same quality category or one or more lower ranking eligibles are better qualified." The objection must be based on a deficiency in the eligible themselves, not a comparison to others. (DEOH Ch.6 D, p. 6-35)
✦ Knowledge Check 2
An objection to an eligible is not sustained. The hiring manager disagrees with the decision. What options does the hiring manager have?
✓ B is correct. When an objection is not sustained, the hiring manager has exactly three options: (1) seek reconsideration by submitting additional information, (2) consider/select the eligible, or (3) decline to make a selection from the certificate. (DEOH Ch.6 D, p. 6-39)

3. Passing Over a Preference Eligible — Basic Rules & Procedures

▼

Definition

A pass-over is an objection filed against a preference eligible that results in the selection of a lower-ranking non-preference eligible, or (under category rating) an eligible in the same quality category who is not preference eligible (DEOH p. 6-40).

Basic Rule

An appointing officer may not pass over a preference eligible to select a non-preference eligible unless they submit reasons sufficient to warrant the pass-over based on proper and adequate reasons (5 U.S.C. § 3318) (DEOH p. 6-40).

Who Has Authority to Rule on Pass-Overs?

DEU Has AuthorityOPM Retains Exclusive Authority
Most pass-overs of preference eligibles (TP, XP, CP with less than 30%) Three categories (DEOH p. 6-34):
1. Medical determinations pertaining to preference eligibles (5 CFR part 339)
2. CPS pass-overs — pass-over of a preference eligible with 30%+ compensable disability (5 U.S.C. § 3318(c)(2))
3. Suitability determinations involving material false statements, fraud, or refusal to furnish testimony (5 CFR § 731.103(a))

Timing Restriction

Pass-overs based on criminal/adverse credit history or OF-306 information may be requested only after a conditional offer has been made (unless an exception was granted) (DEOH p. 6-40).

Excepted Service Applicability

Pass-over procedures apply not only to competitive service positions but also to most excepted service positions that have been excepted by Executive Order or OPM action (per binding case law interpreting 5 U.S.C. § 3320). The exception: positions exempt from part 302 procedures under 5 CFR § 302.101(c). For statutory exceptions, look to the terms of the specific statute (DEOH pp. 6-40, 6-42).

⚠ Exam Trap — Three OPM Retained Authorities: Memorize these three. The exam tests them by presenting a scenario and asking who rules on the pass-over. If it involves a medical condition of any preference eligible → OPM. If it involves a CPS (30%+) veteran for any reason → OPM. If it involves material false statements → OPM for suitability. The DEU handles everything else.
📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.6 Section D, pp. 6-34 and 6-40. The overview of pass-over procedures is on p. 6-34. The distinction between agency-level pass-overs (for TP/CP/XP preference eligibles) and OPM-required pass-overs (for CPS 30%+ only) is on p. 6-40. Key: agency headquarters establishes pass-over procedures for all preference eligibles except CPS 30%+, which requires OPM approval (5 U.S.C. § 3318). The hiring manager must document specific, job-related reasons for the pass-over.
✦ Knowledge Check 1
A hiring manager wants to pass over a TP veteran (5-point preference, no disability) because the veteran lacks a required selective factor that was identified after certification. Who rules on this pass-over?
✓ B is correct. OPM retains exclusive authority only for: medical determinations of preference eligibles, CPS (30%+) pass-overs, and suitability determinations involving false statements/fraud. A qualifications-based pass-over of a TP veteran is within the DEU's authority, in accordance with the delegation agreement. (DEOH Ch.6 D, p. 6-34)
✦ Knowledge Check 2
Under 5 CFR § 332.406, does an applicant who is the subject of a qualifications-based objection or pass-over have a statutory right to appeal?
✓ A is correct. The DEOH states: "Under the provision concerning objections and pass-overs, there is no statutory or regulatory right for applicants to appeal" when the basis is qualifications. However, CPS veterans do have the right to respond to OPM before a pass-over decision is made — that is a different protection. (DEOH Ch.6 D, p. 6-35)

4. CPS Pass-Over — The Four Steps, 15-Day Response & OPM Addresses

▼

The pass-over of a CPS veteran (30%+ compensable disability) has the most rigorous procedural requirements in the entire certification process. OPM retains exclusive authority to grant or deny these pass-overs (5 U.S.C. § 3318(c)(2)).

The Four Steps (DEOH pp. 6-41 to 6-42)

Step 1 — Agency must notify the CPS veteran of the proposed pass-over, including:
• Notice of proposed pass-over with agency, title/series/grade, duty location, certificate number
• Explanation of reasons for the proposed pass-over
• Notice of the right to respond to OPM within 15 days
• The address of the appropriate OPM office

Step 2 — Agency must send to OPM:
• Copy of the pass-over request with supporting documentation
• Copy of the notification sent to the CPS veteran

Step 3 — OPM Official:
• Makes a decision after considering any response from the veteran within 15 days
• Notifies the appointing official AND the veteran in writing

Step 4 — Hiring Manager:
• If sustained → removes the CPS eligible from consideration
• If not sustained → three options: reconsideration, select the eligible, or decline to select from the certificate

Two OPM Addresses — Match to the Basis (DEOH p. 6-41)

Qualifications-Based or Medical-Based ActionsSuitability-Based Actions
U.S. Office of Personnel Management
Employee Services
1900 E Street, NW, Room 6500
Washington, DC 20415		 U.S. Office of Personnel Management
Suitability Executive Agent Programs
Attn: Operations (Adjudications)
P.O. Box 699
Slippery Rock, PA 16057
⚠ Exam Trap — The Two Addresses: The exam tests whether you know which OPM office handles which type of pass-over. Qualifications and medical → Washington DC (1900 E Street). Suitability → Slippery Rock, PA. Sending the request to the wrong address delays the process. The Slippery Rock address is distinctive enough that it's a reliable exam anchor.
📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.6 Section D, pp. 6-40 to 6-44. The CPS 30%+ pass-over four-step process is on pp. 6-41 to 6-42, formatted as a step table: Step 1 (notify CPS veteran with reasons, include right to respond to OPM within 15 days), Step 2 (send documentation to OPM), Step 3 (OPM decides after considering veteran’s response), Step 4 (take action on decision). The OPM addresses for qualifications/medical vs. suitability cases are on p. 6-41. The possible outcomes (sustain, deny, return for more information) are on pp. 6-42 to 6-43.
✦ Knowledge Check 1
A CPS veteran is proposed for pass-over based on suitability concerns (material false statements). To which OPM office must the documentation be sent?
✓ A is correct. For suitability-based actions, the address is Suitability Executive Agent Programs, P.O. Box 699, Slippery Rock, PA 16057. The Washington DC address (1900 E Street) is for qualifications-based and medical-based actions. (DEOH Ch.6 D, p. 6-41)
✦ Knowledge Check 2
How many days does a CPS veteran have to respond to OPM after being notified of a proposed pass-over?
✓ C is correct. "The CPS veteran has the right to respond to the hiring manager's reasons for pass-over by submitting a response to OPM within 15 days of the notification." (DEOH Ch.6 D, p. 6-41)

5. Medical Pass-Overs — Reasonable Accommodation & OPM Authority

▼

When Medical Disqualification Applies

An eligible may be considered medically disqualified when they have a physical or medical (including mental health) condition that will prevent them from performing the full range of essential duties safely and efficiently (DEOH p. 6-36).

Two Critical Rules

  1. OPM retains exclusive authority to make medical determinations pertaining to ALL preference eligibles (5 CFR part 339) — not just CPS, but TP, CP, XP, and all other preference categories (DEOH p. 6-34)
  2. Reasonable accommodation must be assessed first: Before submitting a medical pass-over request, the agency must assess "whether reasonable accommodation can be provided to permit performance of the job despite the condition" (DEOH p. 6-42)
⚠ Exam Trap: Medical pass-over authority belongs to OPM for ALL preference eligibles — not just CPS. Many examinees assume only CPS medical pass-overs go to OPM. That's wrong. The CPS exclusive authority is about all types of pass-overs (including qualifications-based). The medical authority is about all preference eligibles (including TP). Two different rules, both requiring OPM involvement.

Medical Pass-Over Address

Medical pass-overs go to: Employee Services, 1900 E Street NW, Room 6500, Washington, DC 20415 — the same address as qualifications-based actions, NOT the Slippery Rock suitability address (DEOH p. 6-43).

📖 On the exam, open the DEOH to Ch.6 Section D, pp. 6-34, 6-36, and 6-42 to 6-44. Medical pass-over rules: for preference eligibles, OPM retains exclusive authority to make medical qualification determinations (5 CFR part 339). Reasonable accommodation must be considered before a medical pass-over (pp. 6-43 to 6-44). For non-preference eligibles, the agency may make its own medical determination. The medical pass-over of a CPS 30%+ veteran follows the same four-step OPM process as other CPS pass-overs but is sent to the qualifications/medical address on p. 6-41.
✦ Knowledge Check 1
A TP veteran (5-point, no disability) is proposed for medical pass-over because a physical condition prevents performing essential duties. Who has authority to make this medical determination?
✓ B is correct. "OPM retains exclusive authority to make medical determinations pertaining to preference eligibles (5 CFR part 339)." This applies to ALL preference eligibles — TP, CP, CPS, XP, SSP — not just CPS. (DEOH Ch.6 D, p. 6-34)
✦ Knowledge Check 2
Before submitting a medical pass-over request, what must the agency assess?
✓ A is correct. "In considering whether a physical or medical condition will have an impact on an eligible's capacity to perform the job efficiently and safely, you are required to assess whether reasonable accommodation can be provided to permit performance of the job despite the condition." This must happen before submitting the request. (DEOH Ch.6 D, p. 6-42)

6. Illegal Selection — Definition, Lost Consideration & Corrective Action

▼

Definition

An illegal selection occurs when a selection made from a certificate is out of order and results in a violation of the selection rules (DEOH p. 6-45).

Four Conditions for Lost Consideration (DEOH p. 6-46)

All four must be met to constitute a violation of law:

  1. A selection was made from an erroneous certificate
  2. When the erroneous certificate is corrected, the affected eligible moves within reach of selection
  3. When corrected, the selectee moves out of selection range
  4. The misranked eligible meets all qualification requirements for the job

Examples of Out-of-Order Selections (DEOH pp. 6-46 to 6-47)

SystemOut-of-Order Selection Occurs When:
